summary: In the fall of 1918, the Spanish flu pandemic swept over Canada and struck my great-grandfather Wilhelm, who was just a boy on a German Lutheran farm in Manitoba. He became seriously ill with double pneumonia and was given only twenty minutes to live by the town doctor. This harrowing experience led Wilhelm to secretly vow that if he recovered, he would devote his life to God and ministry. The pandemic was a pivotal event that shaped his future and, ultimately, my family's history.

Canada is where my great-grandfather Wilhelm grew up, specifically on a German Lutheran farm in Manitoba. It was in Canada during the 1918 Spanish flu pandemic that Wilhelm fell gravely ill but made a secret pact with God to dedicate his life to ministry if he survived. This country was the starting point of his journey, which eventually led him to migrate to the United States for religious training. Canada’s rural communities and religious culture deeply influenced my family’s roots.

Manitoba was the province in Canada where my great-grandfather Wilhelm lived on a German Lutheran farm when the Spanish flu pandemic hit in 1918. It was there that he became seriously ill and made a secret vow to devote his life to ministry if he recovered. Manitoba’s rural and religious environment shaped his upbringing and the decisions that led him to pursue theological studies in the United States. This place marks the beginning of a significant chapter in my family’s history.

My great-grandfather Wilhelm was raised in a strongly religious German Lutheran community on a farm in Manitoba, Canada. This faith community deeply influenced his values and the secret pact he made during the 1918 Spanish flu pandemic to dedicate his life to God and ministry. The German Lutheran tradition shaped not only Wilhelm’s path but also the religious vocations that followed in my family, including my grandmother’s teaching ministry. Their faith was central to our family’s identity and migration story.

In 1922, Pastor Swechmann from Edmonton, Alberta, visited my great-grandfather Wilhelm’s local parish and preached about the need for ministers. This visit inspired Wilhelm and his cousin Henry Fry to agree to become pastors themselves. Edmonton’s pastor played a key role in encouraging their religious calling, which eventually led them to travel to the United States for theological training. This connection to Edmonton was a turning point in Wilhelm’s life and vocation.

Alberta is the Canadian province where Edmonton is located, and it figures into my great-grandfather Wilhelm’s story through the visit of Pastor Swechmann in 1922. This visit to Wilhelm’s local parish sparked the decision for him and his cousin to pursue ministry. Alberta’s religious leadership helped shape Wilhelm’s path, which led him to migrate south to the United States for further training. The province was part of the broader Canadian context of his early adult life.

Saint Paul, Minnesota, was where my great-grandfather Wilhelm and his cousin Henry traveled in 1922 to attend Concordia College for their ministerial training. After convincing US immigration officials that they were coming to become pastors, they arrived in Saint Paul by train and cab. Wilhelm’s diary from his schooling years there in 1928 shows how deeply committed he was to his vocation, balancing religious studies with athletics. Saint Paul marked the beginning of his formal education and migration experience in the United States.

Concordia College in Moorhead, Minnesota, was the institution where my great-grandfather Wilhelm studied after migrating from Canada in 1922. He and his cousin Henry attended Concordia to train for Lutheran ministry, fulfilling the pact Wilhelm made during the Spanish flu pandemic. Wilhelm’s diary from 1928 reflects his dedication to both his religious training and physical fitness, emphasizing the importance of balancing studies and athletics. Concordia College was central to his formation as a pastor and to the religious legacy in my family.
